summary: Ronacher argues that “self hosted” is muddied when modern tools depend on proprietary cloud runtimes, using Counterscale’s Cloudflare dependency as a case study. He contrasts earlier stacks like LAMP/WAMP, which were self-hostable because they relied on shared, open protocols such as CGI and SQL. Today’s equivalents—serverless runtimes, analytics stores, queues, caches—exist, but lack common, portable protocols and packaging. That gap pushes developers to target proprietary platforms with nicer abstractions, trading portability for ease. He concludes the web needs new, socially agreed protocols and a “SaaS in a box” experience to make small-scale open source software easier to build and host.
key_points: Historically, self-hosting worked because stacks shared open abstractions (CGI, SQL) rather than specific vendors.
Modern open source has the parts for serverless and analytics, but lacks standardized protocols and reusable packaging.
Proprietary cloud platforms win by offering higher-level abstractions that reduce operational burden.
Creating new protocols is more a social/coordination challenge than a technical one.
Lowering the cost of deployment via shared primitives could unlock new open source software.
money_quotes: Is it really self hosted if your only way to deploy it is some proprietary cloud vendor?
What makes LAMP, WAMP and whatever work are a few basic technological choices.
We did not agree on protocols and we definitely did not really have that stuff today in a neatly packaged and reusable thing.
The modern web needs the CGI type protocols for queues, for authentication, for columns stores, for caches etc.
Technology also here is hardly the problem.
CGI in many ways is the original serverless.
Yet maybe over time we can see more of a “SaaS in a box” kind of experience.
key_insight: Self-hosting will stay constrained until the web agrees on portable, high-level protocols for modern primitives the way CGI and SQL once did.
